coredata.dc:description: Chemotherapy combined with immunotherapy represents a promising combination therapy for tumor treatment. The low targeting of chemotherapeutic drugs and the potential immune-related adverse events (irAEs) of immune adjuvants hinder the realization of combination therapy. To solve these problems, we propose to further conjugate immunoadjuvant OVA using boric acid (BA) modified drug-carrying carbon dots, and develop a multifunctional nano drug delivery system for hierarchical targeting of focal sites (RCD@DOX/BA-O). From a mechanistic perspective, based on the pKa of BA, RCD@DOX/BA can couple OVA (1, 3-n-diol) under alkaline conditions and release OVA under tumor microenvironment, which could prevent the occurrence of irAEs. Furthermore, the exposed BA was able to combine with sialic acid (SA) (1, 2-n-diol) under the acidic tumor microenvironment, thereby enhancing its tumor-targeting capabilities. Specially, the immune effect (tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) to M1) of OVA and the tumor targeted enhancement was verified by cell experiments. The treatment effect of RCD@DOX/BA-O was further proved on animal level. Overall, our finding suggests that the synthesized RCD@DOX/BA-O enabling synergistic chemotherapy and immunotherapy through hierarchical targeting to enhance anti-tumor efficacy and reduce irAEs. It is of great significance for targeted immunotherapy and improving the therapeutic effect of tumor.

(G) Weight of the tumors collected after sacrificing the mice on day 10. Expression of IgG (H), TNF-α (I), IL-13 (J) and IL-4 (K) in different groups.

Design and synthesis of RCD@DOX/BA-O for hierarchical targeting of antitumor therapy. (I) Schematic showing design and synthesis of RCD@DOX/BA-O. (II) RCD@DOX/BA-O for synergistic OVA-mediated immunotherapy and DOX-mediated chemotherapy.

head.ce:title: Boric acid functionalized carbon nanotheranostic platform for the hierarchical delivery of immune adjuvants and chemotherapy agents

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemotherapy combined with immunotherapy represents a promising combination therapy for tumor treatment. The low targeting of chemotherapeutic drugs and the potential immune-related adverse events (irAEs) of immune adjuvants hinder the realization of combination therapy. To solve these problems, we propose to further conjugate immunoadjuvant OVA using boric acid (BA) modified drug-carrying carbon dots, and develop a multifunctional nano drug delivery system for hierarchical targeting of focal sites (RCD@DOX/BA-O). From a mechanistic perspective, based on the pKa of BA, RCD@DOX/BA can couple OVA (1, 3-n-diol) under alkaline conditions and release OVA under tumor microenvironment, which could prevent the occurrence of irAEs. Furthermore, the exposed BA was able to combine with sialic acid (SA) (1, 2-n-diol) under the acidic tumor microenvironment, thereby enhancing its tumor-targeting capabilities. Specially, the immune effect (tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) to M1) of OVA and the tumor targeted enhancement was verified by cell experiments. The treatment effect of RCD@DOX/BA-O was further proved on animal level. Overall, our finding suggests that the synthesized RCD@DOX/BA-O enabling synergistic chemotherapy and immunotherapy through hierarchical targeting to enhance anti-tumor efficacy and reduce irAEs. It is of great significance for targeted immunotherapy and improving the therapeutic effect of tumor.

RCD@DOX/BA-O enabled synergistic chemotherapy and immunotherapy through hierarchical targeting to enhance anti-tumor efficacy and reduce irAEs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the main treatment methods for cancer include surgery [1], radiotherapy [2] and chemotherapy, among which chemotherapy is the most widely accepted treatment [3]. However, due to the development of resistance to chemotherapeutic agents, the treatment effect is not ideal [4]. In recent years, many assisting chemotherapy methods (photothermal therapy [5], photodynamic therapy [6] and immune adjuvant [7] et al.) have been developed, among which the introduction of immunomodulators has brought many new hopes (regulation of the immune microenvironment, enhanced cellular immune responses and enhanced the immunogenicity [8]). For example, the introduction of the immunomodulator ovalbumin (OVA) could regulate the transformation of TAMs to M1-like (anti-tumor effect) to inhibit the growth of tumor cells [9].

Nonetheless, with the deepening of research, the combined treatment also presents certain challenge [10]. On the one hand, immunomodulators as a kind of foreign substance, like chemotherapeutic drugs, will also trigger the body's immune response. In extreme cases, it may lead to the irAEs, thereby posing a significant threat to human life [11,12]. Therefore, the precision delivery of immune adjuvants should be raised to the same level as the targeted studies of chemotherapeutic agents [13,14]. On the other hand, the sites of action of chemotherapeutic agents and immunoadjuvants in tumors often do not coincide. In briefly, chemotherapeutic agents need to enter the solid tumor for chemotherapy treatment; whereas immune adjuvants often act on the peripheral infiltrating portion of the solid tumor [15]. Both of these aspects place high demands on the precision-targeted and controlled release of drugs and immunoadjuvants. Therefore, at present, there is an urgent need to find an excellent drug delivery platform that could simultaneously satisfy the tumor targeting of drugs and immunoadjuvants, as to spatio-temporal release.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a carbon nanomaterial, are widely used in the biomedical field due to their excellent properties (photobleaching property [16], surface modifiability [17], biocompatibility [18]). Especially in the field of image-assisted drug delivery, CDs provide more reference data of drug distribution in vivo due to their optical properties [19]. Moreover, with the development of drug carrier platforms, the surface properties of CDs have been more researched and extensively developed [20,21]. Such as, CDs have abundant functional groups on the surface and a large specific surface area [22]. As a result, the aforementioned properties ensure that CDs possess an enhanced capacity for loading a wider range of therapeutic drugs. Furthermore, these properties facilitate multifunctional surface modifications of CDs, thereby meeting diverse requirements for sustained-release, controlled-release, and targeted drug delivery. In contrast to the previous text, for the chemotherapy combined with immune adjuvant therapy of tumors, if CDs are used as the core carrier for construction, the selection of surface ligands is undoubtedly the key to success or failure.

Boric acid (BA) modification not only could regulate the optical properties (the array arrangement prevents fluorescence quenching) of CDs, but also could achieve graded release in the acidic tumor microenvironment (pH-sensitive release) and target of tumors (affinity of BA with sialic acid) [23]. Therefore, when BA utilized as a surface ligand to modify CDs, exhibited a broad and promising application potential. In briefly, in neutral or weakly alkaline environments (pH 8.0), BA readily undergoes tetrahedral dissociation to facilitate esterification reactions. It exhibits rapid formation of borate ester bonds with 1, 3-n-diol groups in aqueous solutions [24]. Conversely under acidic conditions (pH 6.5), borate bond hydrolyzes and easy reacts with 1, 2-n-diol [25]. This adjustable reaction allows BA to be used as a pH response ligand [26]. Interestingly, the popular immuno-adjuvant OVA has a 1, 3-n-diol structure [27]. That is to say, BA's pH responsiveness enables OVA to deliver inside the neutral blood, which solved the irAEs, and to dissociate in the slightly acidic tumor microenvironment (pH 5–7) to achieve the immunomodulation of the tumor site, i.e., TAMs to M1. Moreover, the sialic acid (SA) which highly expressed on the tumor cell surface has a 1, 2-n-diol structure [28,29]. The dissociated and exposed BA was able to further bind to tumor via SA-mediated under the acidic condition, which could increase the targeting and penetration of the tumor [30]. Moreover, the binding of BA to SA hindered the interaction between SA and immunoglobulin-like lectin (Siglec) ligand, thereby inhibiting the immune escape of tumor cells [31].

Herein, a multifunctional nanocomplex RCD@DOX/BA-O based on CDs core and BA surface modification was synthesized, that realized the hierarchical targeting effect of chemotherapy and immunotherapy. The specific synthesis process is as follows: RCD@DOX was prepared based on Rhodamine B carbon dots (RCD) and DOX, which as a nano drug delivery system. BA as surface modification ligand was further introduced to form B-C bonds based on electron-absorbing boron atoms, following obtain RCD@DOX/BA. Then the OVA was conjugated to RCD@DOX/BA, a multifunctional nanocomplex RCD@DOX/BA-O is formed (Scheme 1 ). As shown in Scheme 1, the nanocomplex exhibits three advantages: 1) RCD@DOX/BA-O enables OVA firmly to deliver inside the neutral blood, which solved the irAEs, and to dissociate in the slightly acidic tumor microenvironment to achieve the immunomodulation of the tumor site, i.e., TAMs to M1. 2) The dissociated and exposed RCD@DOX/BA was able to bind to SA that highly express on the tumor surface under the acidic condition, which could increase the targeting and penetration of the tumor, and carry DOX into solid tumor. 3) BA-functionalized nanocarrier allows the hierarchical targeting of immune adjuvants and chemotherapy agents. As expected, a synergistic anti-tumor therapeutic platform based on BA functionalized fluorescent CDs has been successfully synthesized, which could target tumor cell and polarize tumor-associated macrophage. Through hierarchical targeted chemotherapy and immunization, the anti-tumor effect was significantly improved and reduced the systemic side effects of drug and immune response. Therefore, the CDs modified by BA has a good application prospect in combination chemistry and immunotherapy.

Rhodamine B (RhB, AR), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), boric acid (BA, AR) purchased from Cologne Chemicals Co. LTD (Chengdu, China). Ovalbumin (OVA), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36 %–38 %) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were bought from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin (DOX) and indocyanine green (ICG) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Calcein/PI cell viability/cytotoxicity assay kit and 2-(4-amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride (DAPI dihydrochloride) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Beijing, China). APC anti-mouse CD86 and PE anti-mouse CD40 were purchased from BioLegend (California, America). Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, America). Certified fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Shanghai XP Biomed Ltd. (Viva Cell, Shanghai, China). 4 % paraformaldehyde fix solution was obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through the Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3 M cm).

Human hepatocellular carcinomas cell (HepG2), Murine breast cancer 4T1 cell line (4T1) and Murine macrophage cell line (RAW264.7) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). The nude mice (female, 5 weeks) were purchased from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. BABL/c mice (female, 5 weeks) were purchased from Enswell Technology Co., LTD (Chongqing, China). And the animal experimental protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

Rhodamine B (RhB), as a fluorescent dye, was widely used in vitro fluorescence imaging [32,33]. It has been reported that rhodamine B carbon can be prepared by hydrothermal method, and their fluorescence intensity is enhanced [34]. The visible fluorescence carbon dots, RCD, was synthesized by hydrothermal pyrolysis according to our previous procedure [28]. In brief, 96 mg RhB were dissolved in 15 mL NaOH aqueous solution (0.67 M), and then the solution was transferred into poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclaves and heated at 180 °C in an oven for 8 h. After cooling down to 25 °C naturally, the orange mixture solution was precipitated by HCl. Then sediment was obtained under centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 3 min and the brown solid RCD was obtained by drying 3 h at 60 °C.

The RCD@DOX was fabricated through a modified method [35]. In brief, RCD (2 mg/mL) and DOX solution (10 mg/mL) were mixed, string in dark for 24 h at 37 °C. The obtained mixture was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min) and the sediment, RCD@DOX, was obtained.

Firstly, 5 mg RCD@DOX and 1 g BA were dissolved in the flask with 40 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic for 30 min. Then solvent of the mixture solution was removed by rotary evaporation under 60 °C for 30 min and the flaxen solid was obtained in the round-bottom flask, i.e. the final product. The characterizations were carried out via the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The DOX loading capacity and release
ce:section.ce:para:
The water loss of RCD (0.1 mg), RCD@DOX (0.05 mg) and of RCD@DOX/BA (3.84 mg) were measured by the Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), respectively. The drug loading capacity (wt, %) was then calculated using the following equation: wt (%) = [B -(100-B)] ×
A
100 ‐ A
× 100 %. A and B represent total weight loss before and after drug loading respectively.

The DOX released form RCD@DOX/BA was carried out via a simple dialysis method. Specifically, 1 mL RCD@DOX/BA solution containing 0.2236 mg DOX was transferred into dialysis bags (MWCO 1000 D) and sealed. The dialytic devices were put 10 mL in phosphate buffer (0.01 M) pH 7.4 and pH 6.5 to simulate the physiological conditions and cancer cells, respectively, stirring at 37 °C, protected from light. At predetermined time, 1 mL PBS was collected followed by replenishing with an equal volume of fresh medium. Then quantified the released DOX by the fluorescence spectroscopy.

The RCD@DOX/BA composite was modified by OVA via physical absorption by electrostatic interaction. Firstly, the RCD@DOX/BA (5 mg/mL) solution was adjusted to pH 8.0 with NaOH solution. Then, the mixture solution of RCD@DOX/BA and OVA (5 mg/mL) were stirred for 2 h at room temperature. Finally, the RCD@DOX/BA-O particles were separated by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) to remove unbound OVA. The conjugation of OVA was charactered by UV–vis absorption, Zeta potential.

The RCD@DOX/BA-O solution was adjusted pH 6.5 with HCl solution after the obtained RCD@DOX/BA-O particles were redissolved with purified water. The dissociated OVA form RCD@DOX/BA-O solution in acid condition was obtained via centrifugation (6000 rpm, 10 min). Then gel of SDS-PAGE was adopted to prove release of OVA from RCD@DOX/BA-O.

HepG2 and 4T1 cells were cultured in the DMEM/F-12 medium supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (37 °C, 5 % CO2). RAW264.7 cells were cultured in the RPMI-1640 medium with the same condition.

The cytotoxicity studies of DOX, RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA on HepG2 were assessed by MTT. Firstly, cells were subcultured overnight into 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells per well. Next day, cells were treated with each formulation with appropriate concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20 and 40 μg/mL) for another 24 h. Next, the medium in each well was replaced by 100 μL of fresh culture medium with 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL). After incubation for 4 h, the supernatant was discarded, and 150 μL DMSO was added to each well. Then, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. At last, cell viability was evaluated by measuring the absorbance of the reaction products at 490 nm using a microplate reader. Every experiment was conducted three times.

HepG2 cells were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well for 24 h. The cells were treated with RCD@DOX/BA equivalented to 10 μg/mL DOX for 4 h. The cells were fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution prepared in PBS for 2 h and then dehydrated. Cells without RCD@DOX/BA treated as a control. The surface morphology of the cells was observed under the SEM.

HepG2 cells were seeded in 24-well plates at a density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well in a 24-well plate prior to incubation with RCD@DOX or RCD@DOX/BA (10 μg/mL of DOX). After incubation with each formulation for 4 h, the cells were washed by PBS three times and fixed by 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min. The cells were stained with DAPI for 10 min twice and washed by PBS three times. The fluorescence intensity of each formulation finally was observed under the inverted fluorescence microscope.

Three Five-week female nude mice were injected 100 μL HepG2 cells (5 × 105 cells) suspended with PBS under the armpits of mice. After the subcutaneous tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3, the mice were injected with RCD@DOX/BA@ICG. In time (0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h), small animals living imager was used to observed fluorescence distribution in mice. And the animal experimental protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

HepG2 cells were inoculated in 12-well plates at a density of 5 × 105 cells per well for 24 h. After co-incubation with RCD or RCD/BA for different times (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 12 h and 24 h), the cells were collected into 1.5 mL EP tubes, washed three times with PBS and resuspended with 150 μL PBS solution. Cellular uptake behavior at predetermined time was detected by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro polarization of macrophages into M1-like macrophages
ce:section.ce:para: RAW264.7 cells were cultured with the RPMI-1640 medium at a density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well in a 24-well plate, and stimulated with LPS (25 ng/mL), RCD@DOX/BA (10 μg/mL of DOX), OVA (40 μg/mL) or RCD@DOX/BA-O (40 μg/mL of OVA) for 12 h, respectively. Then cells were collected using 1.5 mL tubes through centrifuge (1800 rpm, 5 min). Each tube was strained with 100 μL staining buffer solution containing APC-CD86 and PE-CD40 (antibody:staining buffer = 1:100) for 40 min in dark at 4 °C. Then cells were fixed by 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min and washed by PBS three times and cells then were resuspended with 150 μL PBS. The polarization of macrophages was finally measured by flow cytometry.

RAW264.7 cells were incubated with OVA (40 μg/mL), RCD/BA (100 μg/mL) or RCD/BA-O (40 μg/mL of OVA) at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h in a 24 well-plate. Then cells were collected and resuspended with RPMI-1640 medium. HepG2 cells and resuspended RAW264.7 cells were simultaneously seeded in a 24 well-plate for 12 h. Then, dead and living cells were collected to strain with Calcein AM/PI test solution for 40 min under dark condition. Fluorescence images (Calcein AM, λex/em = 494/517 nm and PI, λex/em = 535/617 nm) were observed via the inverted fluorescence microscope.

Five-week female nude mice were subcutaneously injected 100 μL HepG2 cells (5 × 105 cells) suspended with PBS. After the subcutaneous tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3, model mice were randomly divided into 4 groups, 5 mice for each group. Mice were injected with PBS, DOX, RCD@DOX or RCD@DOX/BA at an equivalent dose of 5 mg/kg (DOX/body weight) via the tail-vein every 2 days interval [36]. Tumor size and body weight were monitored every day for 2 weeks by a digital caliper and a digital balance, respectively. The tumor volume was calculated as follows:
tumor

/
mm 3
)

length ×
width 2
2
After 14 days, mice were euthanized, the tumor sites and the main organs (heart, liver, spleens, lung, kidney) were harvested for H&E staining.

Five-week female BABL/c mice were subcutaneously injected 100 μL 4T1 cells (1 × 106 cells) suspended with PBS. After the subcutaneous tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3, model mice were randomly divided into 5 groups, 6 mice for each group. Mice were injected with PBS, DOX, OVA, RCD@DOX/BA and RCD@DOX/BA-O at an equivalent dose of 5 mg/kg (DOX/body weight) or 2.5 mg/kg (OVA/body weight) [37] via the tail-vein every 2 days interval. Tumor size and body weight were monitored by a digital caliper and a digital balance, respectively. The tumor volume was calculated as follows:
tumor
volume

)

length ×
width 2
2

After treatment, mice were euthanized, the tumor sites and the main organs (heart, liver, spleens, lung, kidney) were harvested for H&E staining. Serum was collected for the detection of inflammatory factor TNF-α, tumor immunomodulatory factors IL-13 and IL-4 and immunoglobulin IgG and IgE.
The animal experimental protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

The hematotoxicity of RCD@DOX/BA-O was evaluated to verify whether it can be injected intravenously. Briefly, fresh whole blood obtained from KunMing (KM) mice was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The plasma and buffy coat layer located in the supernatant were discarded, and the remaining red blood cells (RBCs) were further washed using PBS (pH 7.4) until the supernatant became colorless. 5 % RBCs solution were obtained with RBCs suspended in PBS. 1 mL RBCs suspension mixed with 1 mL normal saline (NS) (0 % hemolysis) and 1 mL ultrapure water (100 % hemolysis) was set as negative control and positive control, respectively. The sample groups consist of 1 mL RBCs suspension, 800 μL NS and 200 μL RCD@DOX/BA-O (0.1, 0.5 and 1 mg/mL). All groups were placed at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the absorbance of each group was measured at 545 nm using a microplate reader.

Six female nude mice were divided into two group, then each group were injected PBS and RCD@DOX/BA via tail-vein respectively. Collecting the whole blood successively, mice serums were obtained from whole blood via centrifugation under 6000 rpm for 5 min. The level of liver function's makers (ALT, AST, ALP) and kidney function's makers (UA, CREA, BUN) were measured in mice serums.
The animal experimental protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 13 software and the results were presented as the mean ± S. D. Student's t-test was used to compared differences between groups. The statistical significance was determined at the level of p < 0.05.

Based on rhodamine B (RhB) have good fluorescent propertise, RCDs was made by hydrothermal pyrolysis of RhB according literature reported [38]. Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 1 A, the drug delivery system (RCD@DOX/BA) was constructed via loading DOX and further modification with BA according our concept. Specially, BA was decomposed under high temperature conditions (180 °C) into B2O3 with oxygen negative ion, which provided the binding site for disease therapy. Fig. 1B showed the absorption spectra of RCD, RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA, respectively. The characteristic peak of DOX located at 480 nm which could be observe from the absorption of RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA. The absorption peak of BA was prominently observed in the UV spectrum following BA modification, indicating the successful incorporation of BA. Interestingly, the characteristic peak of DOX was blue shift to some extent because hydrophobic forces may cause the shift or superposition of spectra [39]. And the similar phenomenon was also reported in previous literatures [29,40]. Fig. S1 shows the color difference of different groups of powders, it could be clearly observed that RCD@DOX has increased red compared with RCD group. RCD@DOX/BA has the weakest red color compared to the other groups due to further modification by BA. Insert image showed their solution further recorded under white light manifested that RCD@DOX was successful synthesized and modified with BA. Subsequently, the fluorescence properties before and after loading DOX and modification with BA were investigated. PL spectra in Fig. 1C showed that fluorescence intensity of RCD decreased after loading DOX, as well as the characterization peak of DOX appeared (λem = 480 nm). In addition, a new fluorescence emission peaks located at around 550 nm appeared in the PL spectra of RCD@DOX/BA compared with that of RCD@DOX. The insert image was that fluorescence images of those formulations recorded under UV lamp with excitation wavelength at 365 nm. Above all, those results demonstrated that RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA were successful fabricated. We further investigated the morphology of RCD and RCD@DOX/BA via TEM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1D, most of RCD possess spherical shape and the size was around 11 nm characterized by its TEM result, as well as the morphology of RCD@DOX/BA was near-spherical, homogeneous particles in Fig. 1E. However, the size distribution of RCD@DOX/BA was increased to around 13 nm compared with that of RCD, indicating that the introduction of DOX and BA were significantly increased the size of the nanoparticles.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the Zeta potential of RCD was −12.06 ± 3.15 mV, while RCD@DOX/BA reversaled to 15.13 ± 1.35 mV. In our previous study found that BA modified RCD is also negatively charged [41], and the reason for the positive charge shown here should be caused by DOX loading. This phenomenon further proved the successful loading of DOX and modification with BA. By comparing the IR spectra of BA, RCD and RCD@DOX/BA in Fig. 2B, the characteristic peaks of RCD@DOX/BA at 3226 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of O-H and B-O, respectively [42]. The peak at 1189 cm−1 is the asymmetrically stretched oxygen atom connected to the trigonometric boron atom, and these characteristic peaks are all derived from the involvement of BA [43]. The new band at 791 cm−1, which is characteristic of a B-C bond [44], further confirms the bonding between RCD and BA. The above characteristic peaks together indicate the successful synthesis of RCD@DOX/BA. The elemental analysis of the prepared RCD@DOX/BA was further evaluated by XPS, the XPS spectra of the RCD@DOX/BA in Fig. 2C exhibited B 1 s peak at 195 eV, C 1s peak at 286 eV and O 1s peak at 534 eV [45,46], which indicated that the main elements of RCD@DOX/BA contained boron, carbon and oxygen. Next, we analyzed the XPS high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, B 1s of RCD@DOX/BA. Fig. 2D showed that the C 1s XPS spectrum has three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.8 eV and 288.7 eV which were ascribed to C-C/C-N, C-O and C=O, respectively [47,48]. Meanwhile, Fig. 2E presented the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s band could be fitted into three peaks at 533.3 eV, 533.4 eV and 535.6 eV, which belong to C-O band, B-O band and -COOH group, respectively, indicating that the oxygen-containing groups were present on surface of RCD [49] and the existence of BA [44]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2F, the high-resolution B 1s spectrum of RCD@DOX/BA exhibited three peaks at 192.5 eV, 193.9 eV and 194.7 eV for B-C, B-O and B2O3, respectively [50]. Above characterizations revealed the successful fabrication of RCD@DOX/BA.

Subsequently, the capacity of loading drug of RCD@DOX/BA was calculated. Since the absorption overlapped before and after loading DOX, the UV–vis method couldn't be taken consider, and the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) further was adopted to evaluate the DOX loading capacity. Fig. S2A–C show comparative thermogravimetric analysis curves of RCD alone, RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA with total masses of 0.15 mg, 0.005 mg, 3.84 mg and final weight loss ratios of 45.04 %, 75.75 %, 48.82 %. As Table 1 exhibited, the drug loading (wt%) of RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA were around 55.88 % and 6.88 % respectively following calculation results. Next, the DOX release efficiency of RCD@DOX/BA under different conditions was discussed, simulated physiological condition (pH 7.4) and acidic TME (pH 6.5). The establishment of a DOX fluorescence standard curve was utilized to quantify the release quantity of DOX at different time points (Fig. S3A). As shown in Fig. S4, compared to pH 7.4, cumulative drug release of DOX is 132 μg/mL at pH 6.5 within 72 h. However, up to 150 μg/mL of DOX was released at pH 6.5 during the same time period, indicating that RCD@DOX/BA could respond to the change of pH to control DOX release.

Next, we further fabricated the RCD@DOX/BA-O and the schematic illustration was demonstrated in Fig. 3 A. Briefly, ovalbumin (OVA) was conjugated to RCD@DOX/BA at alkaline environment (pH 8.0) and dissociated at acid condition (pH 6.5). Subsequently, the behavior of OVA conjugation and dissociation at different condition were assayed. The UV–vis spectra in Fig. 3B displayed that the peak located at the wavelength of 281 nm was attributed to the characteristic absorption peaks of OVA. A new peak at the wavelength of around 491 nm appeared, ascribing to the coupling of OVA with RCD@DOX/BA under alkaline conditions (pH 8.0). This may be attributed to the covalent labeling of OVA with a fluorophore (rhodamine). And the local conformational alteration of the protein induced by the labeling process resulted in the modification of the absorption spectrum [51,52]. Conversely, the peak disappeared under acid conditions (pH 6.5) due to the successful dissociation of OVA from RCD@DOX/BA, indicating that the couple reaction was reversible. The PL spectra in Fig. 3C showed that the fluorescence of RCD@DOX/BA-O significantly increased compared with RCD@DOX/BA and the emission located at 541 nm disappeared. The reason could explain that the successful coupling of OVA destroys the characteristic peak of RCD@DOX/BA (λem = 541 nm). We further investigated the morphology of RCD@DOX/BA-O via TEM. As shown in Fig. S5, most of RCD@DOX/BA-O possess spherical shape and the lattice spacing is 0.2354 nm, which corresponds to the lattice of 221 by its TEM result. Zeta potentials then were measured to further confirm the as-construct. As shown in Fig. 3D, the zeta potential changed from 15.1 ± 1.3 mV reversal to −17.97 ± 1.56 mV before and after OVA conjugation. Among zeta potential of OVA alone is −7.64 ± 0.61 mV. Surprisingly, zeta potential was up to 7.48 ± 0.80 mV when RCD@DOX/BA is placed at acidic condition (pH 6.5). The interesting phenomenon ascribed to reversibility of coupling reactions. In addition, to further confirm the successful synthesis of RCD@DOX/BA-O, OVA profiles of RCD@DOX/BA-O was determined by SDS-PAGE electrophoresis assay and Coomassie blue staining. As shown in Fig. 3E, purified RCD@DOX/BA-O (pH 8.0) and OVA had the same total protein expression spectrum, both located around at 45 kDa, which manifested the successful conjugation of OVA. Interestingly, RCD@DOX/BA-O supernatant (pH 6.5) and RCD@DOX/BA-O sediment (pH 6.5) also had the expression of OVA, but the protein expression of RCD@DOX/BA-O sediment (pH 6.5) was lower than RCD@DOX/BA-O supernatant (pH 6.5), revealing that part of OVA dissociated from RCD@DOX/BA-O under acidic condition. Those results showed that the RCD@DOX/BA-O was successful fabricated at alkaline condition and the conjugation of OVA was reversible under acid condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ability of RCD@DOX/BA to target tumor cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before evaluating the targeting efficacy of RCD@DOX/BA and antitumor ability of RCD@DOX/BA-O in tumor cells, we assessed their biosafety and biocompatibility in vitro via MTT assay. Firstly, MTT assay was carried out via HepG2 cells or RAW264.7 cells respectively. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, three groups exhibited different degrees of cytotoxicity with predetermined DOX concentrations (0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL and 40 μg/mL). The HepG2 cell survival rates for free DOX, RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA were approximate 80 %, 60 % and 70 % respectively when the DOX concentration reached 10 μg/mL. With the concentration increasing to 20 μg/mL, the cell survival rate for the free DOX group decreased to less than 60 %, while the ability of the RCD to promote drug accumulation resulting in cell survival rate approximately decreased to 32 % and 43 % for the RCD@DOX and RCD@DOX/BA group respectively. When The DOX concentration reaching to 40 μg/mL, all cells survival rates were below 40 %. Considering the antitumor efficiency, we chose the 20 μg/mL DOX as the following experiments. As well as, the best concentration of OVA was also confirmed according the same method. As showed in Fig. S3B, the OVA loading of other groups was quantified by establishing the standard curve of free OVA. RCD@DOX/BA-O with different concentrations (0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL) incubated with RAW264.7 cells. The results in Fig. S6 manifested that RCD@DOX/BA-O, as same with free OVA (control), had almost no effect on cell survival in this concentration range, probably because OVA is one of the high-quality proteins in egg white, a typical albumin, which contains amino acids that can be nutrients for cell normal growth. Hence, 40 μg/mL OVA was selected as the concentration for subsequent experiments.

Subsequently, the behavior of cellular uptake was monitored from three aspects. We first investigated the morphology of cells before and after cellular uptake RCD@DOX/BA via the scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 4B, compared with the PBS group (control), the surface of cell with RCD@DOX/BA treated was rough and bumpy, ascribing to the oxygen negative ions on the RCD@DOX/BA specifically bind to SA highly expressed on HepG2 cells. Making it more targeted to the tumor cells and resulting in significant change for cell morphology. The fluorescent images of RCD@DOX or RCD@DOX/BA were further observed (Fig. 4C). Those images revealed that RCD fluorescence (green signal) and DOX fluorescence (red signal) were in cytoplasm and nucleus respectively. Both of fluorescence intensity of RCD@DOX/BA was much higher than that of RCD@DOX, exactly as the quantitative analysis of fluorescence shown in Fig. S7, manifesting that BA modification played a vital role in increasing DOX accumulation in HepG2 cells. Moreover, the capacity of intracellular uptake was explored by flow cytometry. Noteworthy, considering the fluorescence band overlapped between RCD and free DOX, we determine to direct evaluate the cellular behavior of RCD or RCD/BA. As displayed in Fig. 4D, the intracellular uptake level of RCD/BA was supreme at 2 h at predetermined time points (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h), followed by cell efflux, which was accordance with the quantitative analysis of uptake. Nevertheless, RCD group (control) displayed a consistent uptake rate. Quantization results were shown in Fig. S8. All the above results demonstrated that RCD@DOX/BA could perform excellent uptake and targeting ability.

Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) are actively recruited to tumors and cause immunosuppression microenvironment. M1-type macrophages, a type of polarized macrophages, had an antitumor effect based on production of TNF-α. More importantly, the expression of anti-CD40 and anti-CD86 were increased when macrophages polarized into M1-type macrophages. OVA, usual as an exogenous antigen in the cancer immunotherapy, was selected to construct nano-vaccine (RCD@DOX/BA-O) based on RCD@DOX/BA. Next, we evaluated the role of RCD@DOX/BA-O in tumor immunotherapy. The effect of RCD@DOX/BA-O on the tumor-associated macrophage polarization was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, RAW264.7 treated with LPS or OVA was as control, displaying M1-type macrophage expression of 93.95 % and 79.92 % respectively. Compared with RCD@DOX/BA group (25.75 %), RAW264.7 treated with RCD@DOX/BA-O showed a significant increase to M1-like TAMs expression of 91.73 %, following an equivalent expression level of LPS group. The results were consistent with quantitation analysis of CD40/CD86 presented in Fig. 5B. Moreover, Western blot (WB) indicated that RCD@DOX/BA-O increased more secretion of cytokine TNF-α from RAW264.7 compared with PBS or RCD@DOX/BA, which was comparable to that of LPS group (Fig. 5C). This further confirmed that the RCD@DOX/BA-O played a crucial role in the regulation of TAMs polarization. In addition, we further examined the antitumor capacity of M1-like TAMs via Calcein/PI cell viability/cytotoxicity assay kit. Considering the presence of DOX could affect the Calcein/PI channel fluorescence, we chose the formulation without DOX. RAW264.7 cells treated with different formulations were fed to HepG2 cells, then, Calcein AM/PI co-stained HepG2 cells. The result was demonstrated in Fig. 5D. Among, control group only contained HepG2 cells, without RAW264.7 feeding. The quantity of live cells (green fluorescence signal) treated with RCD/BA-O was equivalent to free OVA and both were significantly less than other groups. In contrast, that of dead cells (red fluorescence signal) increased compared with other treatments. Above phenomenon could ascribe to antitumor effect of M1-like TAMs induced by RCD/BA-O. Hence, RCD/BA-O could as a tumor vaccine to regulate the polarization of TAMs and further enhance immune response and improve antitumor efficiency.

Based on the discoveries above, the chemotherapy and immunotherapy effect of RCD@DOX/BA-O in vivo was investigated next. Before the study of therapeutic effect, the tumor targeting of the RCD@DOX/BA in mice was studied. ICG was uploaded on the basis of RCD@DOX/BA for in vivo imaging in mice. Fig. 6 A showed the UV spectra of the free ICG, RCD@DOX/BA and RCD@DOX/BA@ICG. The coincidence of the absorption peaks of the free ICG and RCD@DOX/BA@ICG at 780 nm proved the successful upload of the ICG. HepG2 cells bearing nude mice was chosen as a model, as shown in Fig. 6B, when the tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3, the mice were tail-vein injected with RCD@DOX/BA@ICG. Fluorescence imaging was performed on mice at predetermined time points (0 h, 0.5h, 1h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h) to observed the distribution of RCD@DOX/BA@ICG in the mice, and the results showed that RCD@DOX/BA@ICG had good tumor targeting ability.

And then we started a chemotherapy study for tumors, as shown in Fig. 6C, HepG2 cells were injected subcutaneously into mice to establish tumor model in vivo. When the tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3 and mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (n = 5). The mice were tail-vein injected with different preparations every 2 days seven times, respectively. The photos of mice were recorded on day 4, 10, 14 respectively during administration (Fig. 6D). After administration, the mice were sacrificed and tumors were collected in Fig. 6E, seen that the growth of tumors in mice treated with RCD@DOX/BA was significantly reduced compared with the RCD@DOX or free DOX treatment, suggesting that the antitumor effect of RCD modified with BA improved significantly because of the special bind between BA and SA. Meanwhile, the antitumor effect of RCD@DOX was slightly higher than that of free DOX treatment group, explaining that RCD was developed based on RhB carbonization, as a drug carrier, further facilitating drug delivery. Furthermore, H&E-stained sections of major organs (Fig. S9) confirmed that RCD@DOX/BA did not induce substantial damage to normal organs. H&E-stained sections of tumor tissues (Fig. 6F) revealed that tumors in the PBS group exhibited lower differentiation and more severe deterioration compared to those in the other groups. Meanwhile, tumor volumes were also monitored during administration and the tumor growth profiles as shown in Fig. 6G indicated that RCD@DOX/BA had the best inhibitory effect. While other treatments had inferior efficacy, which illustrated that the result was in accordance with Fig. 6E. RCD@DOX/BA could markedly inhibit tumor growth and reduce the tumor size (Fig. 6H). Moreover, RCD@DOX/BA had little effect on the body weights of mouse (Fig. 6I). Meanwhile, ALT, AST and ALP markers of liver function in RCD@DOX/BA group (Fig. S10A–C) and kidney function markers (UA, CREA and BUN) in Fig. S10D–F were not statistically different from PBS group. Manifesting the negligible toxic side effects of RCD@DOX/BA.

After demonstrating the chemotherapy efficacy in vivo, we next investigated the immunotherapy of RCD@DOX/BA-O in vivo. Here, we chose 4T1 tumor-bearing BABL/c mice as the tumor mode and the scheme was shown in Fig. 7 A. Hemolysis tests conducted on RCD@DOX/BA-O prior to drug administration revealed (Fig. S11) that RCD@DOX/BA-O did not induce hemolysis of red blood cells, thus confirmed its suitability for in vivo administration in mice. When the tumor volume reached 50–100 mm3 and mice were randomly divided into 5 groups (n = 6). Different preparations were injected into the tail vein of mice every 2 days for a total of three times. Mice were sacrificed at an interval of 5 days after the last dose. The photos of mice were recorded on day 1, 3, 5, 10 respectively during administration (Fig. 7B). The body weight of mice during the treatment period was recorded, as shown in Fig. S12, and it was found that the body weight of mice in different groups did not change significantly. After administration, the mice were sacrificed and tumors were collected in Fig. 7C and D, seen that the growth of tumors in mice treated with RCD@DOX/BA-O was significantly reduced compared with the PBS or free DOX treatment, suggesting that chemotherapy combined with immunotherapy enhanced the efficacy of antitumor therapy. Meanwhile, tumor volumes were also monitored during administration and the tumor growth profiles as shown in Fig. 7F indicating that RCD@DOX/BA-O had the best inhibitory effect. While other treatments had inferior efficacy, which illustrated that the result was in accordance with Fig. 7C. The results of tumor tissue weighing further demonstrated that RCD@DOX/BA-O exhibited a superior tumor treatment effect (Fig. 7G). Furthermore, the results of H&E staining of the tumor tissue were presented in Fig. 7E. In the PBS group, there were obvious bubbles in the tumor sections because a large amount of oil appeared in the tumor tissue after deterioration and suppuration. As illustrated in Fig. S13, no significant lesions were observed in the normal tissues across all groups, indicating a favorable safety profile for the formulations. Serum samples from mice in various groups were collected for ELISA analysis of relevant immunoglobulin and anti-inflammatory markers, with the results presented in Fig. 7H–K. As the immunoglobulin with the highest expression in serum, the expression of IgG in the OVA group was the highest due to the occurrence of systemic immune response, and there was a significant statistical difference compared with the RCD@DOX/BA-O group (Fig. 7H). This result demonstrated that the targeted release of OVA significantly reduced the incidence of systemic immune responses. IgE is an immunoglobulin expressed at a very low level in serum. Therefore, no significant difference could be statistically determined between the OVA and RCD@DOX/BA-O groups (Fig. S14). However, the expression of IgE in the OVA group was still slightly higher than in the RCD@DOX/BA-O group. The occurrence of a systemic immune response could also lead to an increase in inflammatory factors. Hence, the level of TNF-α in the OVA group was significantly elevated compared to other groups. However, the concentration of TNF-α in the RCD@DOX/BA-O group was markedly reduced compared to the OVA group, attributed to the targeted release mechanism of OVA and the mitigation of the systemic immune response (Fig. 7I). In addition, gene transduction studies confirmed that IL-13 has both specific and non-specific antitumor activities. IL-4 has entered phase Ⅱ clinical trials as a tumor immunomodulator. OVA has the least expression of IL-13 and IL-4 due to the onset of systemic immune inflammation and reduced tumor therapeutic activity. While, the expression of RCD@DOX/BA-O was significantly higher than that of OVA (Fig. 7J and K). These findings suggest that RCD@DOX/BA-O is capable of diminishing systemic immunity and achieving immune modulation at the tumor site through the regulated release of OVA.

To further verify that RCD@DOX/BA-O regulated the targeted release of OVA to alleviate the occurrence of systemic immunity. The mouse model and treatment were replicated with precise control over the therapeutic dose of DOX. Fig. S15A–H shows the treatment results when the effective dose for controlling DOX is 5 mg/kg.The photos of mice were recorded on day 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 respectively during administration (Fig. S15A). During the treatment period, the mice in the OVA, DOX, and RCD@DOX/BA groups exhibited weight loss starting from day 9 (Fig. S15F). The weight loss observed in the OVA group could be attributed to a systemic immune response, whereas the weight loss in the DOX and RCD@DOX/BA groups was primarily due to the toxic side effects associated with the DOX. The growth trend of tumors in mice during the treatment period was subjected to a statistical analysis (Fig. S15E). The findings indicated a significant difference in tumor volume between the PBS group and the RCD@DOX/BA-O group, attributable to the synergistic effect of chemotherapy and immunotherapy in the latter. After administration, the mice were sacrificed and tumors were collected in Fig. S15B. It is evident that, in comparison to the PBS group, the other groups exhibited a certain degree of tumor treatment efficacy and effectively inhibit tumor progression. Tumor tissue weight also further demonstrated the tumor inhibition effect of each group, among which the RCD@DOX/BA-O group was statistically different from the PBS group (Fig. S15H). The death of mice in each group during tumor treatment was further illustrated by Fig. S15B and G. It could be seen that the largest proportion of mice in the OVA group died due to the systemic immune side effects of OVA, and the RCD@DOX/BA-O group reduced the occurrence of systemic immune side effects due to the targeted release of regulated OVA, and the mortality rate was significantly controlled. Finally, the heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor tissues of different groups of mice were collected for H&E staining (Fig. S15C and D). The results indicated that the PBS group was unable to inhibit tumor growth. The tumor exhibited an irregular shape, a low degree of differentiation, and a more severe extent of deterioration. Due to the severe inflammatory reaction in the OVA group, more oil appeared in the tumor tissue after suppuration. In addition, the spleen of the OVA group showed obvious lesions and loss of splenic bodies, which were caused by the systemic immune response to OVA. In conclusion, the present study demonstrated that RCD@DOX/BA-O mitigated the occurrence of systemic immune side effects and reduced mortality by regulating the targeted release of OVA. In addition, the combined treatment of chemotherapy and immunity enhances the therapeutic effect of tumors.

In summary, we successfully construct a fluorescent nanocomposite RCD@DOX/BA with the BA functionalization as a targeted drug delivery, which further conjugated OVA to develop the RCD@DOX/BA-O for synergistic antitumor therapy based on active targeting tumor cells and positioning release OVA for immunotherapy. RCD@DOX/BA-O could respond to the acidic environment of the tumor (pH 5–7), and BA breaked down under acidic conditions to release OVA to achieve tumor targeted immunotherapy of OVA. BA specially bind to SA, that overexpressed on tumor cells, allowing the DOX accumulate at site of tumor and preventing immune escape from the tumor. We also discussed the targeted ability of RCD@DOX/BA via three different methods (SEM, fluorescence imaging and flow cytometric) and anti-tumor effect in vivo was demonstrated by mice subcutaneous tumor models. Meanwhile, RCD@DOX/BA-O could induce polarization of TAMs to M1 macrophages, further enhance anti-tumor effect. Hence, this study has the versatility of BA-functionalized nano-medicine carriers, which allows hierarchical targeted therapy of chemotherapy and immunity, providing more possibilities for precise antitumor therapy.

Tao Deng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
